coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous collapsing encapsulated carbon dots: Direct evidence for the effect of multiple-confined interactions of silica on efficient long-lived phosphorescence

coredata.dc:description: Coupling with a matrix is the common strategy to improve the phosphorescence performance of carbon dots (CDs). However, there is a lack of clear and direct evidence about the formation mechanism of the inner relationship between CDs and matrix, and the specific effect of matrix on the phosphorescence performance. Mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) were used as the research model to provide the direct evidence of the dense structure formation process and to construct a quantum chemical theoretical computational model of the effect of dense matrix on phosphorescence performance. Due to the multiple domain-limiting effects of rigid Si-C covalent bonds and SiO2 dense structure, the calcined-carbonized polymer dots-mesoporous silica nanospheres (C-CPDs-MSNs) can exhibit thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) by tuning the HOMO-LUMO gap and enriching the electrons in the surface region. With a long lifetime of 3.07 s, the composite can also maintain stable phosphorescence performance under harsh conditions and has been successfully applied in multilevel information encryption, fingerprint identification, and cellular imaging. This work provides the direct evidence for the process of dense SiO2 structure enhancing the phosphorescence performance of CDs and offers a general strategy and method for the design and preparation of efficient and practical CDs-based phosphorescent materials.

head.ce:title: Mesoporous collapsing encapsulated carbon dots: Direct evidence for the effect of multiple-confined interactions of silica on efficient long-lived phosphorescence

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coupling with a matrix is the common strategy to improve the phosphorescence performance of carbon dots (CDs). However, there is a lack of clear and direct evidence about the formation mechanism of the inner relationship between CDs and matrix, and the specific effect of matrix on the phosphorescence performance. Mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) were used as the research model to provide the direct evidence of the dense structure formation process and to construct a quantum chemical theoretical computational model of the effect of dense matrix on phosphorescence performance. Due to the multiple domain-limiting effects of rigid Si-C covalent bonds and SiO2 dense structure, the calcined-carbonized polymer dots-mesoporous silica nanospheres (C-CPDs-MSNs) can exhibit thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) by tuning the HOMO-LUMO gap and enriching the electrons in the surface region. With a long lifetime of 3.07 s, the composite can also maintain stable phosphorescence performance under harsh conditions and has been successfully applied in multilevel information encryption, fingerprint identification, and cellular imaging. This work provides the direct evidence for the process of dense SiO2 structure enhancing the phosphorescence performance of CDs and offers a general strategy and method for the design and preparation of efficient and practical CDs-based phosphorescent materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have the advantages of long-lived luminescence properties and high signal-to-noise ratio in the time dimension, and have great potentials in anti-counterfeiting, multi-level information encryption, biological imaging, etc [1–6]. As a new type of photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of good photostability, high biocompatibility, and simple preparation process [7–9]. CDs-based nanomaterials have been expected to replace traditional long-afterglow materials, such as luminescent materials of the organometallic complexes and compounds of the rare-earth metals [10,11].

However, under light excitation, the exciton transition process of CDs is affected by spin prohibition, resulting in a lack of triplet excitons, and the occurrence of non-radiative transition processes leads to the deactivation of triplet excitons and external quenching factors under the influence of water and oxygen, causing the short phosphorescence lifetime and low phosphorescence quantum yield (QY) from the original CDs, both of which further result in the rare occurrence of phosphorescence phenomenon [12–14]. Since CPDs carry multiple polymer chains on their surfaces or have polymer-like structures, these rigid substrates are able to form a self-protecting effect, resulting in efficient RTP emission. Liu's group used PVA and ethylenediamine to prepare a carbon-dot RTP material with a graphitized carbon core and rich polymer-chain structure covering the surface under hydrothermal conditions [15]. Yang's group used polyacrylic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials to obtain a polymer carbon dots with excellent RTP properties by hydrothermal reaction with crosslinking enhanced emission [16]. Lin's group reported a phosphorus doped carbon dots with similar polymer structure protective layer, which was realized by gradual heating from fluorescence to long-lived phosphorescence [17]. Feng's group prepared fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with self-protecting and ultra-long RTP lifetimes by hydrothermal method using a two-solvent system [18]. Besides, heteroatoms (N, P, F, B, etc.) generally can be introduced to the CDs system to promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) process [9,17,19–21]. Besides, CDs can be anchored/embedded into a matrix such as PVA, urea, cyanuric acid, boric acid, alumina, silica and so on, in which CDs can be inhibited by the stabilization of chemical bonds or the spatial confinement of the rigid matrixes [22–27]. Moreover, the rotation and vibration of CDs can be suppressed to some extent and surface groups also can reduce the energy dissipation caused by non-radiative transition processes [13,28]. These methods were generally applied to achieve the long-lived and bright phosphorescence in CDs-based field.

In recent years, coupling CDs with a silica matrix has become a common strategy to realize the efficient phosphorescence behaviour [20,29–35]. Lu et al. constructed a dense silica matrix composite of CDs-based phosphorescent material through silane hydrolysis and high-temperature calcination, showing a lifetime of 2.11 s and a phosphorescence QY of 36.68 % [26]. Sun et al. adopted the sol–gel method to embed CDs into a highly rigid silica matrix, resulting in a CDs-based composite with an ultra-long afterglow lifetime of 5.72 s and a phosphorescence QY of 26.36 % [22]. Shan′s group encapsulated CDs into a silica matrix and prepared a CDs-based composite material with an afterglow lifetime of 1.86 s and a phosphorescence QY of 11.6 % [30]. The excellent RTP performance of these materials is achieved through both the covalent bonding function between CDs and the SiO2 matrix, and the dense framework, but the reported works failed to disclose the visual evidence for the formation process of these dense structures. In addition, Yang′s group achieved water-soluble afterglow in mesoporous silica modified with amino functional groups through covalent bond grafting of CDs, but the lifetime was only 176.9 ms and the regular morphology of MSNs (mesoporous silica nanospheres, some abbreviations and corresponding full names were listed in Table S1) is simultaneously disrupted [36]. Xu′s group coupled CDs with hollow MSNs via disulfide bonds, while the corresponding composite only emits near-infrared fluorescence without phosphorescence action [37]. Wang et al. doped CDs into mesoporous silica during the stirring process, which also only shows fluorescence emission under the confinement of mesoporous channels [38]. It can be seen that both the covalent bonding function between CDs and silica, and a dense structure from CDs/silica are the key to construct CDs-based RTP materials with long lifetime and high QY in the silica matrix. However, it has a crucial impact on the RTP emission of CDs-based materials that searching the efficient methods to visualize the process of how the substrate encapsulates CDs, and it can provide direct evidence for the formation of multiple-confined interactions between CDs and the matrix, and this is an important issue that need to be further investigated in depth.

In this study, MSNs were used as carriers. With the help of hydrothermal reaction, poly acrylic acid (PAA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) in the mesoporous channels were cross-linked, polymerized and carbonized to form CPDs. Simultaneously, the MSNs channels collapsed and CPDs were encapsulated in-situ into collapsed mesoporous pore walls. Moreover, high-temperature calcination converted part of the Si-O-C bonds into more rigid Si-C covalent bonds and densified the loose silica matrix. Owning to the stabilizing effect from covalent bonds and the protection of dense silicon-oxygen network, the C-CPDs-MSNs exhibit an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 3.07 s, a phosphorescence QY of 22.93 %, as well as TADF characteristic. It can also maintain excellent phosphorescence emission and stable RTP lifetime in strong oxidizing reagents, pH = 1–13 solutions, and a series of polar solvents. In addition, a comparative study was carried out that most of the ordered structure of MSNs was still retained and the as-prepared calcined-normal temperature stirring-carbonized polymer dots-mesoporous silica nanospheres (C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs) showed weak phosphorescence emission when the CPDs failed to form a tight structure with the silicon-oxygen network in MSNs, and the corresponding phosphorescence lifetime was shortened to 1.74 s. Herein, it was demonstrated that the formation of rigid Si-C bonds and dense Si-O networks between CDs and silica substrates is the key to achieving excellent phosphorescence properties. Based on the excitation dependence, super stability, excellent photoluminescence, and monodispersity of C-CPDs-MSNs, these composites were successfully applied in multi-level information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, highly sensitive fingerprint detection, and cellular imaging.

The preparation process of C-CPDs-MSNs was shown in Fig. 1 a. PAA and EDA are incorporated into the pores of MSNs. The pore structure gradually collapses at the duration of forming CPDs under hydrothermal treatment, and the CPDs are in-situ encapsulated in the pore wall in silica matrix. Then high-temperature calcination densifies the loose SiO2 matrix and forms a dense combination with the CPDs, activating the obtained C-CPDs-MSNs with ultra-long RTP. Referring to a reported work with some modifications, CPDs were hydrothermally reacted at 200 °C for 8 h using PAA and EDA as reactants, then dialyzed with a dialysis bag with the molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1000D in deionized water to purify the CPDs solution (Fig. S1a) [16]. Fig. S2e shows the particle size distribution of CPDs with the average size of 3.28 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [39]. The CPDs stock solution was stirred with MSNs for 8 h to encapsulate CPDs into the MSNs pores, in which this mild treatment can keep the pores from collapsing as shown in Fig. S1b.

The morphology of MSNs and MSNs/CPDs-based composites was characterized as shown in Fig. 1, Fig. S1, and Fig. S2. As-prepared MSNs have a highly regular spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution, with the particle size distribution around 120 nm in Fig. 1b [40]. High-resolution SEM images (Fig. S3) indicate MSNs are full of pore structure, which is conducive to the adsorption of PAA, EDA and CPDs into the pore channels. The monodispersing and regular spherical morphology were maintained for both the mesoporous CPDs-MSNs (carbonized polymer dots-mesoporous silica nanospheres) doped with CPDs via high-temperature hydrothermal treatment and the mesoporous NTS-CPDs-MSNs (normal temperature stirring-carbonized polymer dots-mesoporous silica nanospheres) doped with CPDs by normal-temperature stirring treatment, and the corresponding particle size distribution is ∼ 120 nm as shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. S1c, respectively. After calcination at 600 ℃ for 2 h, both the C-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs can still maintain their highly regular spherical morphology (Fig. 1d, Fig. S1f). TEM images display that the MSNs have the pore structure as shown in Fig. 1e and Fig. S2a. Fig. 1f shows that after high-temperature hydrothermal treatment, the pore structure of the CPDs-MSNs collapsed seriously, and the distribution of CPDs (black particles) on the surface of the CPDs-MSNs was observed in high-resolution TEM image (Fig. S2b). However, the NTS-CPDs-MSNs doped with CPDs were stirred at normal temperature, in which most of the ordered pore structure can well preserve (Fig. S1d). CPDs (black particles) were dispersed on the surface of NTS-CPDs-MSNs in HRTEM image (Fig. S1e), indicating that the mild stirring process successfully doped CPDs into the MSNs [36]. After calcination at 600 ℃ for 2 h, C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs can still maintain most of the ordered pore structure (Fig. S1g), but it is difficult to recognize the distribution of CPDs (Fig. S1h). In contrast, the pore structure of the C-CPDs-MSNs collapsed (Fig. 1g), and a distinct distribution of CPDs was observed in the collapsed pore wall matrix (Fig. S2c). The particle size of CPDs in C-CPDs-MSNs is ∼ 2.5 nm with the lattice space of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite as shown in Fig. S2d [39]. Fig. S2f shows the ′CPDs with a particle size of 3.23 nm, similar to the particle size of CPDs (Fig. S2e), and both of them have the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [39]. Based on the above experimental results, it can be reasonably assumed that during the stirring process of MSNs and CPDs at normal temperature, the MSNs channel structure only partially collapses. Due to physical adsorption, CPDs can be successfully doped into the mesoporous channels to obtain NTS-CPDs-MSNs. For NTS-CPDs-MSNs, most of the CPDs are physically embedded into the channels and fail to form a strong combination with the silica matrix. During high-temperature calcination, lots of CPDs are consumed due to their exposure to the channels. During the hydrothermal treatment, the CPDs are simultaneously formed as the pore channels collapse, and the CPDs are in-situ encapsulated in the mesoporous pore wall in silica matrix (like a sandwich). The CPDs-MSNs can protect a large number of CPDs from destruction when calcined at high temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct evidence for building dense structures
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the pore collapse after the hydrothermal treatment or the calcination, nitrogen adsorption isotherms were actualized on MSNs, CPDs-MSNs, C-CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs, as well their specific surface areas and pore structures. The MSNs sample exhibits type IV isotherm (Fig. 2 a), which is consistent with the characteristics of mesoporous materials [41]. In the adsorption curve of MSNs, the P/P0 value is 0.28, indicating that the synthesized MSNs have obvious regular mesopore arrangement [42]. The specific surface area (SBET) of MSNs was calculated to be 1349.5 m2/g, the average pore diameter was 2.5 nm, and the total pore volume was 0.85 cm3/g, indicating that the synthesized MSNs are capable of loading CPDs.

Both NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs show type IV isotherms as shown in Fig. S4b, and Fig. 2c, respectively. They are consistent with the characteristics of mesoporous materials. For NTS-CPDs-MSNs, the P/P0 value is 0.15, the specific surface area (SBET) is 57.6 m2/g, the average pore diameter is 6.0 nm, and the total pore volume is 0.086 cm3/g. In comparison with MSNs, the possible reason for the decrease in specific surface is the process of stirring and doping CPDs at normal temperature causes the pores of mesoporous silica to be filled with CPDs particles [38]. After high-temperature calcination, the P/P0 value of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs is 0.28, the specific surface area (SBET) is 411.9 m2/g, the average pore diameter is 3.2 nm, the total pore volume is 0.33 cm3/g, and the specific surface area is larger than that of NTS-CPDs-MSNs. The reason for the rise is that the high temperature can impel the CPDs to fill in the pores. The pore size distribution and adsorption isotherm are similar to those of MSNs and conform to the mesoporous type IV isotherm, which illustrates that the process of stirring and doping CPDs at normal temperature, and the calcining treatment can still keep the composites having the mesoporous structure from destruction.

The CPDs-MSNs also exhibit type IV isotherm (Fig. S4a), indicating that CPDs-MSNs can still maintain mesoporous structure after experienced the hydrothermal process. The calculated P/P0 value is 0.22, the specific surface area (SBET) is 37.5 m2/g, the average pore diameter is 5.5 nm, and the total pore volume is 0.05 cm3/g. The specific surface area is greatly reduced compared with the initial sample (MSNs), indicating that the hydrothermal process caused collapse of the pore structure. As shown in Fig. 2b, the adsorption isotherm of C-CPDs-MSNs conforms to the type IV isotherm and still maintains part of the mesoporous structure. The corresponding P/P0 value is 0.15, the specific surface area (SBET) is 40.8 m2/g, the pore size distribution is ∼ 10 nm, the pore size distributed at ∼ 80 nm, and the total pore volume is 0.04 cm3/g, indicating the collapse of mesoporous channels. Calcination causes the pore channels to further collapse, and the specific surface area increases slightly compared with CPDs-MSNs. The possible reason is that part of the CPDs in the pores are consumed after high-temperature calcination. The corresponding data comparison of these composites were listed in Table S2.

As shown in Fig. 2d, the synthesized MSNs have a prominent diffraction peak (100) at 2θ = 1.9° in the low-angle XRD pattern, indicating that the prepared MSNs is orderly arrangement [43]. Hexagonal structure with mesophase of p6 mm hexagonal symmetry [44,45]. After the hydrothermal reaction, the diffraction peak of CPDs-MSNs near 2θ = 1.9° completely disappeared, indicating that the hydrothermal reaction caused the mesoporous channels to collapse and destroyed the orderly arrangement of MSNs. In addition, C-CPDs-MSNs also show the disappearance of this order after high-temperature calcination (Fig. 2e). However, the low-angle XRD of NTS-CPDs-MSNs under normal temperature stirring conditions still shows the existence of weak diffraction peaks near 2θ = 1.9° (Fig. 2d), indicating some ordered structure was still maintained. The low-angle diffraction peak of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs after further calcination near 2θ = 1.9° is stronger than that of NTS-CPDs-MSNs. It is possible that calcination causes the CPDs in the pores to be consumed and the ordered structure of some mesoporous silica pores to be restored (Fig. 2e). However, the weaker diffraction peak near 2θ = 1.9° is found for both NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs comparing to MSNs, which may be due to the collapse of some ordered structures at the duration of doping CPDs and filling the mesoporous channels with CPDs. The density measurements indicates that calcination treatment increased the density, the corresponding values for CPDs-MSNs, C-CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs are 2.31, 3.13, 2.25 and 2.75 g⋅cm−3, respectively (Fig. 2f). It is proved that the silica collapsed during the calcination process as a result of a denser structure [46].

Seen from the results of BET tests, low-angle XRD data, and density measurements, it can be proved that hydrothermal process can cause pore channels tend to collapse, orderliness gradually gets deteriorated at the formation process of CPDs-MSNs. At the same time, CPDs are closely combined with the collapsed mesoporous pore walls. Further high-temperature calcination leads to the formation of C-CPDs-MSNs via the dense silica-oxygen network coupling with CPDs. Through the confinement effect of the rigid silica network, the vibration of the functional groups on the CPDs surface is suppressed, the energy dissipation is reduced from the non-radiative transition, and the ultra-long afterglow of C-CPDs-MSNs is activated [46]. The process of embedding CPDs into MSNs through stirring at normal temperature can retain most of the ordered structure of the mesopores. However, it is precisely by the presence of the ordered mesoporous, the extent of the collapse is slight, and CPDs are loosely attached to the MSNs channels and fail to form a tight combination with the silica matrix. During the high-temperature calcination process, since NTS-CPDs-MSNs lack the effective protection from silica matrix, the CPDs exposed in the pores are consumed in large quantities. TGA curves of CPDs-MSNs and NTS-CPDs-MSNs display the changes in carbon content during the calcination process as shown in Fig. 2g. When the temperature increased from 150 °C to 350 °C, the mass of CPDs-MSNs remained almost unchanged, while the mass of NTS-CPDs-MSNs decreased by ∼ 10 %. High-temperature calcination consumes a large number of CPDs in NTS-CPDs-MSNs [27], resulting in the weak luminescence intensity and shortened phosphorescence lifetime from C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs.

EPR measurements were conducted to further demonstrate that the CPDs in NTS-CPDs-MSNs failed to be effectively combined with the silica matrix, resulting in a large number of CPDs being consumed during the high-temperature calcination process. EPR spectra of C-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs can characterize their stable triplet state. As shown in Fig. 2h and Fig. 2i, the EPR signals from C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs after the illumination of 0 min and 5 min were lower than that from C-CPDs-MSNs, indicating that the luminescence center from C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs is much less comparing to C-CPDs-MSNs [22]. Therefore, the CPDs in CPDs-MSNs are greatly confined in the Si-O networks, and the spatial protection and confinement effect of the dense silica network can protect CPDs from being destroyed under calcination at 600 °C. However, for NTS-CPDs-MSNs, the CPDs are attached in the mesoporous channels and fail to form a tight combination with the SiO2 matrix. Under high-temperature calcination, CPDs are seriously consumed, and the number of luminescence center is largely reduced, resulting in the relatively poor phosphorescence from C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs.

In order to further identify the chemical interaction between CPDs and MSNs for the treatment of hydrothermal method and normal temperature stirring, the CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, C-CPDs-MSNs, and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns.

FTIR curves of CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, C-CPDS-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs were shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3d. The broad band at 3206–3570 cm−1 is stretching vibration of the O-H bonds [20]. The band at 1513–1718 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C = O bond [36]. The bands at 979–1343 cm−1, 743–866 cm−1 belong to Si-O-C and Si-C covalent bond, respectively [33]. FTIR results reveal that there are obvious Si-O-C and Si-C covalent bond interactions between CPDs and MSNs, and they are conducive to forming a rigid and stable structure within the CPDs and the silica network, which is beneficial to restrain the process of nonradiative transition. By comparing the infrared spectra before and after the calcination, it can be clearly seen that the intensity of the hydroxyl peak and C = O decreased after calcination. It is reasonable to assume that part of the CPDs and silanol groups are consumed after calcination. After high-temperature calcination, the Si-C bond strength in C-CPDs-MSNs is significantly stronger than that in C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs, suggesting that pore collapsed to realize the encapsulation of CPDs in-situ, leading to the formation of more and strong rigid covalent bonds between MSNs and CPDs.

XPS overall survey indicates that these four MSNs/CPDs-based nanocomposites are mainly composed of the elements C, Si, O, and N as shown in Fig. 3g, and the peaks at 284.8 eV, 102.9 eV, 532.0 eV, and 399.5 eV belong to C1s, Si2p, O1s, and N1s, respectively [33]. The N1s peak of the C-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs composites almost disappeared, and the C1s peak weakened after the calcination. This is because the CPDs are consumed during the high-temperature calcination process, and part of the C and N dissipated in the form of gas [46]. High-resolution O1s (Fig. S5) can be fitted as follows: CPDs-MSNs: C = O (532.3 eV), Si-O (532.8 eV); NTS-CPDs-MSNs: C = O (531.8 eV), Si-O (532.5 eV); C-CPDs-MSNs: C = O (532.3 eV), Si-O (532.9 eV); C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs: C = O (532.1 eV), Si-O (532.9 eV), Si-O-C (534.9 eV) [20]. The high-resolution N1s spectra of CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, and C-CPDs-MSNs at 399.1 eV, 400.0 eV, and 401.5 eV belong to Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectra of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs at 399.5 eV, 401.7 eV, and 402.6 eV belong to Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N, respectively (Fig. S6) [46].

In Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c, the binding energies of 284.4 eV, 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.5 eV in the XPS high-resolution C1s spectra of CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, C-CPDs-MSNs, and NTS-CPDs-MSNs can be corresponded to Si-C, C-C/C = C, and C-O-Si and C = O bonds [27]. The high-resolution Si2p spectra of CPDs-MSNs, NTS-CPDs-MSNs, and C-CPDs-MSNs can be fitted to Si-C (102.4 eV), Si-O-C (103.2 eV), and SiO2 (103.8 eV), respectively [47]. The high-resolution Si2p spectra of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs at 102.6 eV, 103.7 eV and 104.8 eV were attributed to Si-C, Si-O-C, and SiO2 bonds, respectively (Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f) [29]. By comparing the high-resolution Si2p spectra of these four MSNs/CPDs-based nanocomposites before and after calcination, it can be found that the content of Si-C bond increased significantly after calcination. By comparing the high-resolution C1s spectra before and after calcination, it can be seen that the C-O-Si bond content decreased seriously after calcination (Fig. 3h). As shown in Fig. 3i, the XRD patterns of the four composite materials have broad peaks at ∼ 22°, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [48]. Therefore, it can be shown that after the high-temperature calcination process, part of the C-O-Si bonds in the composites transformed into Si-C bonds. The calcination process is conducive to the increase of Si-C content, which leads to the formation of a more rigid covalent bonding structure, inhibiting the vibration of the functional groups on CPDs and suppressing the energy dissipation caused by the non-radiative transitions from the CPDs [46].

It can be concluded that pore structure of MSNs collapsed and CPDs were simultaneously doped into the collapsed channels at the duration of hydrothermal treatment, and a loose structural combination is formed within CPDs and MSNs. Further calcination process can convert the loose structure into a rigid one [30]. The 3D silica network firmly anchors the CPDs into silica matrix via the function of Si-C covalent bonds [49]. Due to the rigid matrix′s limitation effect on the group vibration of CPDs and the isolation effect from external quenching factors, as well as the stabilizing effect of Si-C bonds, it is greatly suppressed that the vibration energy from the emitters of CPDs. Based on these multiple limitation effects, the domain effect enables C-CPDs-MSNs to exhibit long lifetime and long-duration luminescence. During the process of stirring and doping CPDs at normal temperature, the collapsed mesoporous channel is rare, the silica network and the CPDs fail to form a tight combination, and the amount of Si-C covalent bond formation is deficient, failing to form a tight bond with the CPDs. The silica network promotes to form the tightly packed CPDs, which are gravely consumed during the high-temperature calcination process, resulting in the reduction of luminescence centers, low luminescence intensity, and short lifetime of C-NTS-CPDs-MSN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, to determine the optimum calcination temperature of the material. The optical properties of C-CPDs-MSNs prepared at different temperatures (400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700 °C) were studied. Fig. S7 shows the lifetime fitting curves, PL emission spectra, and FL emission spectra of C-CPDs-MSNs after calcination at 500, 550, and 650 °C. C-CPDs-MSNs prepared by calcination at 500, 550, and 650 °C have different phosphorescence lifetimes but possess similar phosphorescence and fluorescence emission peak positions. As shown in Fig. S8a–c, the phosphorescent 2D excitation-emission plot of C-CPDs-MSNs prepared by calcination at 500 °C, 550 °C, and 650 °C, respectively. All of them show the strongest emission peaks near 470 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelengths are both near 250 nm and 320 nm. As shown in Fig. S8d, C-CPDs-MSNs calcined at 600 °C showed the strongest emission intensity near 470 nm, while the samples calcined at 400 °C, 450 °C, and 700 °C had almost no PL intensity. There is almost no phosphorescence emission due to the fact that the lower temperature retains a large number of CPDs under calcination at 400 °C and 450 °C, causing the high concentration annihilation characteristic, while the high temperature of 700 °C consumes a large number of CPDs, these results are consistent with previous studies [27]. The optimal excitation wavelength of the samples calcined at different temperatures is ∼ 320 nm (Fig. S8e), and the optimal phosphorescence lifetime is 3.07 s when calcined at 600 °C (Fig. S8f). The phosphorescence lifetimes for calcination at 500 °C, 550 °C, and 650 °C were 1.14 s, 1.98 s, and 2.25 s, respectively. Therefore, the optimized calcination temperature is 600 °C for C-CPDs-MSN for the further investigation. As shown in Fig. S9 and S10, the optical photographs of the samples were taken at 254 nm, 365 nm UV lamp on and off state, respectively. Among them, the 1.5-C-CPDs-MSNs showed light blue fluorescence under 254 nm excitation and cyan phosphorescence after the excitation stopped. The 0.5-C-CPDs-MSNs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation and a bright cyan afterglow after the excitation source was turned off. The remaining samples all showed blue fluorescence under 254 nm excitation and blue phosphorescence after stopping the excitation, and all showed light blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation and green phosphorescence after stopping the excitation. The average lifetimes of 0.5-C-CPDs-MSNs, 1.5-C-CPDs-MSNs, 2.0-C-CPDs-MSNs, 2.5-C-CPDs-MSNs can be calculated to be 2.18, 2.03, 1.77, and 1.94 s, respectively (Fig. S11). All of them are lower than the phosphorescence lifetime of 3.07 s for the C-CPDs-MSNs, and therefore, it is determined that the selection of 0.36 g PAA and 350 μL EDA doping.

In addition, as shown in Fig. S12, CPDs, CPDs-MSNs, and NTS-CPDs-MSNs possessed similar FL optical properties, and the optimal emission wavelengths of FL were all located near 400 nm, indicating that the MSNs were successfully complexed with CPDs. Secondly, the optical properties of C-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs were comparatively studied. Fig. S13 shows the optimal emission wavelengths for both C-CPDs-MSNs and C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs are ∼ 470 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelengths are located at 310–320 nm. Fig. S14 displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CPDs, CPDs-MSNs, and NTS-CPDs-MSNs, it can be observed that the energy band at ∼ 260 nm originates from the π-π* transition of C = C. The absorption band at a wavelength of 330 nm can be assigned to the n-π* transition associated with C = O [46].

As shown in Fig. S15, the optimal PL emission peak of MSNs is near 436 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength is near 243 nm, which is inconsistent with the optimal emission (470 nm), and excitation wavelength (320 nm) of C-CPDs-MSNs. The PL lifetime of the MSNs at 243 nm excitation and 436 nm emission wavelengths is 0.33 s. Keeping the same test conditions, under 320 nm wavelength excitation, the MSNs only exhibit extremely weak PL intensity, which is barely visible to the naked eye, while the C-CPDs-MSNs possess super strong PL emission. Therefore, the strong PL emission and long lifetime of C-CPDs-MSNs originate from CPDs constructed from PAA and EDA rather than MSNs.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, C-CPDs-MSNs exhibit blue fluorescence when excited by 254 and 365 nm UV lamps, and show blue and green afterglow respectively after UV lamp is turned off. Fig. 4b and Fig. 4c are the phos.-2D excitation-emission plot of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs, respectively. The optimal excitation wavelength for C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs is 300 nm, and the strongest emission peak is located at ∼ 460 nm. C-CPDs-MSNs has the strongest emission peak at 470 nm under excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The phosphorescence color changes of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs are shown in the CIE (Commission International d′Éclairage) 1931 coordinate diagram (Fig. 4d). The corresponding RTP color coordinates for C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs are (0.206, 0.257) and (0.193, 0.238), both of them locating at blue emission region. The decay curves for time-resolved afterglow spectroscopy can be well fitted with a triple exponential function, from the decay components, the average lifetime was calculated according to the equation:
τ

α i
τ i 2
/ ∑

τ i
The calculated lifetimes of C-CPDs-MSN and C-NTS-CPDs-MSN were 3.07 s and 1.74 s, respectively (Fig. 4e and Fig. 4f). The phosphorescence intensity of C-CPDs-MSNs is much higher than that of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs as shown in Fig. 4g. The QY of C-CPDs-MSNs is 22.93 % (Fig. S16).

The emission shoulder at 430 nm gradually appeared with increasing temperature, while the emission band at 470 nm gradually weakened. Therefore, there has a TADF component with a thermally activated effect in the 430 nm emission and a phosphorescent component in the 470 nm emission (Fig. 4h). Meanwhile, based on the low-temperature (77 K) fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of C-CPDs-MSNs, the energy gap (ΔEST) can be quantitatively determined to be 0.12 eV (Fig. 4i). Due to such narrow energy gap, electrons are easy to scamper from T1 to S1 in the reverse intersystem. As a result, the TADF nature of C-CPDs-MSNs was activated under the stimulation of an external heat source. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of C-CPDs-MSNs was found to be 7.8 ns (Fig. 4j).

These results suggest that CPDs in a failure of tight coupling with silica network tend to be seriously consumed, causing a reduction of PL center, weak PL intensity, and a shortened lifetime of C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs. While for C-CPDs-MSNs samples, they experienced the hydrothermal treatment and further calcination. At these durations, the pore channels collapsed and CPDs are in-situ encapsulated in the silica matrix, the binding function between CPDs and silica network is quite strong and closely, and those shortcomings can be overcome, demonstrating strong PL emission and ultra-long lifetime, as well as TADF property. This is attributed to that the CPDs were caught in rigid silica matrix, which can efficiently avoid the quenching factors from the surrounding environment, and the stabilizing effect of the Si-C covalent bond and the spatial confinement effect of the matrix can inhibit the vibration of surface group of CPDs, which further reduces the energy dissipation caused by the non-radiative transition process of triplet excitons [49].

The absence of phosphorescence in the CPDs-MSNs (Fig. 5 a) can be attributed to the partial Si-O-C bonding between the CPDs and the loosely packed silica matrix (Fig. 5b), which results in a relatively weak coupling between the CPDs and the matrix, and fails to effectively bind the vibrations of the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. The triplet excitons dissipate most of their energy in the form of non-radiative leaps, leading to the phosphorescence bursts. Upon exposure to high temperature (600 °C, 2 h), parts of the Si-O-C bonds in the matrix were transformed into more stable Si-C covalent bonds (Fig. 5f), which not only enhanced the chemical stability of the matrix, but also resulted in a more rigid silica network. This rigidized structure promotes the effective binding of triplet excitons by C-CPDs-MSNs and improves the radiation attenuation rate, ultimately achieving excellent phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) properties (Fig. 5e).

In addition, the HOMO-LUMO distributions of CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs are illustrated in Fig. 5d and Fig. 5h, respectively. The HOMO-LUMO gap (ΔE = ELOMO-EHOMO) is considered to be a key parameter reflecting the optical properties [50–52]. High-temperature calcination triggered a compacting of the silica-oxygen network structure, resulting in a significant reduction of the HOMO-LUMO gap from 3.380 eV for CPDs-MSNs to 2.244 eV for C-CPDs-MSNs. This change suggests that calcination narrows the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of C-CPDs-MSNs and enhances the orbital coupling of HOMO-LUMO, thus potentially increasing the probability of ISC and RISC processes.

The quantum chemical computational models of CPDs-MSNs and C-CPDs-MSNs are shown in Fig. S17a, b, respectively. In Fig. 5c and Fig. 5g, the distribution of molecular surface Electrostatic potential (ESP) demonstrates different surface areas, with the red areas indicating ESP positive values and the blue areas indicating ESP negative values. It can be significantly observed that calcination leads to the breaking of some C-O-Si bonds, resulting in the formation of more rigid Si-C bonds, making the molecule more compact as a whole. This calcination-induced structural change makes it possible that the electron enrichment in the surface region. As a result, the transition probability of electrons from the excited state to the ground state in C-CPDs-MSNs is significantly increased, which effectively enhances the intensity of phosphorescence emission. In summary, the optical properties of C-CPDs-MSNs are significantly improved via tuning the HOMO-LUMO gap and enriching the electrons in the surface region. This model study provides insights into the effects of the multiple domain-limiting effects of the dense silica-oxygen structure and the rigid Si-C covalent bonds on the phosphorescence properties of CPDs, and it also provides a significant support for the further optimization of the optical properties of CPDs.

In addition, the stability of the ultra-strong phosphorescence emission of C-CPDs-MSNs was further studied. The outer layer of C-CPDs-MSNs is protected by a rigid Si-O network, which protects CPDs from being quenched by surrounding environments and improves the stability. Fig. S18- Fig. S20 show the optical photographs of C-CPDs-MSNs under excitation of 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps (“on” and “off”) in strong oxidizing agents, polar solvents, and pH = 1–13 solutions, respectively. C-CPDs-MSNs can show ultra-long and stable PL emission. C-CPDs-MSNs were sonicated with H2O2 (30 wt%), HNO3 (30 wt%) and H2SO4 (98 wt%) for 5 min [27]. Although the phosphorescence intensity decreased, there are still ultra-stable phosphorescence lifetimes (Fig. 6 a, Fig. 6d). After treated with different polar solvents, C-CPDs-MSNs still can show stable luminescence performance and afterglow lifetime, such as in anhydrous ethanol, methanol, DMF, methylene chloride, cyclohexane, ethyl acetate, and petroleum ether (Fig. 6b, Fig. 6e). When C-CPDs-MSNs were placed in solutions with pH = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13, the luminescence intensity and afterglow lifetime were almost unchanged (Fig. 6c, Fig. 6f). These results prove that the CPDs grown in the MSNs channels and collapsed in-situ, enabling the CPDs to form a tight combination with the silica network. The constructed dense structure of the silica protects the strong PL of CPDs from quenching, demonstrating efficient stability against surrounding quenching factors (Fig. 6g), leading to the ultra-long afterglow lifetime and strong optical stability of C-CPDs-MSNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Information security, fingerprinting and cellular imaging applications
ce:section.ce:para: Due to C-CPDs-MSNs have different PL color under 254 and 365 nm excitation, and samples calcined at different temperatures show different PL lifetime. Based on these two features, C-CPDS-MSNs calcined at different temperatures were applied in advanced anti-counterfeiting, multi-level information encryption, high-sensitivity fingerprint detection, and cellular imaging. In Fig. 7 a, petals 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 correspond to C-CPDS-MSNs, C-CPDS-MSNs-650, C-CPDS-MSNs-550, C-CPDS-MSNs-500 and CPDs-MSNs respectively. It can be observed that under the excitation of 254 nm UV lamp, all petals show bright blue fluorescence. After the excitation is cut off, petal No. 5 fails to show PL. The blue phosphorescence from petals No. 4 and No. 3 disappeared successively. Under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, all petals showed bright blue fluorescence. After the excitation stopped, petal No. 5 fails to show phosphorescence. Over time, the green phosphorescence from petals No. 4 and No. 2 gradually disappeared. In Fig. 7b, numbers 1, 1, 0, 1, and 1 (from left to right) are shaped with C-CPDS-MSNs, C-CPDS-MSNs-650, C-CPDS-MSNs-550, C-CPDS-MSNs-500 and CPDs-MSNs, respectively. Under 254 nm excitation, the binary code “11011” is displayed, which can be converted to a decimal value of “27”. After turning off the UV lamp, the “110” blue code sequence is displayed, which should be converted to a decimal value of “6”. While at 365 nm excitation wavelength, the code “11011” is displayed. After the UV lamp turned off, the green code “101” is displayed, indicating the decimal value of “5”. As shown in Fig. 7c, the blue FL of C-CPDS-MSNs can show clear fingerprint features and has the potential to be used in fingerprint detection. C-CPDS-MSNs displays blue and green PL under 254 and 365 nm UV lamps, respectively, and samples calcined at different temperatures have different PL duration properties, making C-CPDS-MSNs have broad application prospects in advanced anti-counterfeiting and multi-level information encryption.

MTT assay was performed on C-CPDs-MSNs using HeLa cells to evaluate their cytotoxicity prior to biological application. As shown in Fig. S21, the concentration of C-CPDs-MSNs in the range of 0.025–0.8 mg/mL, the cell viability keeps more than 80 % after incubation for 48 h, indicating that C-CPDs-MSNs had relatively low biotoxicity and could be used for biological applications. To evaluate the potential application of C-CPDs-MSNs in bioimaging, HeLa cells were incubated with C-CPDs-MSNs (0.1 mg/mL) for 12 h, and then washed 3 times with PBS buffer solution, and their luminescence images were recorded on a confocal microscope as shown in Fig. 7d and Fig. 7e. The blue emission signals of the cells could be detected in the range of 415 nm to 450 nm, and intense green emission signals also can be gathered after gate delay of 8.5–12 ns in the range of 450–550 nm. It also can be found that C-CPDs-MSNs are uniformly distributed in the cytoplasm of HeLa cells and possess the potential for afterglow bioimaging.

In summary, this work proposes a solution to visually demonstrate the impact of a tight network between CPDs and the matrix on their phosphorescence properties. Part of the mesoporous ordered structure is maintained in C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs, causing the silica matrix to fail to form a tight combination with the CPDs. During the high-temperature calcination process, the CPDs are seriously consumed, resulting in decreased PL centers. The PL of CPDs exposed to the environment is susceptible to be quenched, causing C-NTS-CPDs-MSNs to exhibit extremely weak phosphorescence signal and a phosphorescence lifetime of only 1.74 s. While the CPDs are formed during the hydrothermal process, the pores of MSNs collapse, and the CPDs are encapsulated in the collapsed mesoporous pore walls (SiO2) in-situ. The high-temperature calcination can transform the Si-O-C bonds into more rigid Si-C covalent bonds. A further increase in the structural density of the silica network promotes the formation of a tighter bond between CPDs and the substrate, and reduces the energy escape of non-radiative leap processes, which leads to an extension of the phosphorescence lifetime of 3.07 s, the QY of 22.93 %, and the trigger of TADF phenomenon. Due to the protection function from a highly rigid SiO2 matrix, C-CPDS-MSNs can maintain excellent RTP performance and stable RTP in strong oxidizing agents, acids and alkalis, and polar solvents. Taking advantage of the stable phosphorescent properties of C-CPDS-MSNs, which were successfully applied in multi-level information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, high-sensitivity fingerprint detection as well as cellular imaging. This work can promote the development of CDs-based phosphorescent materials and expand their application scopes.
